Florida, as part of the Sunbelt region of the U.S., is naturally appealing for any bank. But before I turn bullish again, I'm going to have to see some concrete results.

data.title: The Market Can Still Climb This Wall Of Worry - But Not Yet
data.description:
Trying to predict President Trump's next step in the war and trade based on that makes no sense to me.

That said, the recent headlines have not been particularly encouraging for the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz. Some sources suggest that Trump may be willing to end the war in the near term, without reopening the Strait of Hormuz.

At the same time, Trump made the following remark (addressed to the U.S. allies, particularly in Europe) in one of his Truth Social posts, which is anything but reassuring from a Strait of Hormuz reopening perspective:

You’ll have to start learning how to fight for yourself, the U.S.A. won’t be there to help you anymore, just like you weren’t there for us. Iran has been, essentially, decimated. The hard part is done. Go get your own oil!

It's not that I disagree with his remarks. What makes me concerned is the market reaction to that post. Here is the market tape for March 31 (pre-market, which is when I'm writing this piece):

Pretty much everything is in the green, across all sectors and all U.S. benchmarks. The VIX is down, and so are the Brent and WTI futures.

Call me crazy on this one, but given that there is no progress on reopening the Strait of Hormuz (which is the main pressure on the U.S. equity markets since the start of the war), this feels like a dead cat bounce.

Needless to say, I'm not buying the dip yet, and I reiterate my 6,150 target for the S&P 500 in H1 this year.

In this piece, I will zoom out and look beyond the war headlines. I will discuss my view on what I consider one of the largest bricks in the wall of worry that the market will have to climb in the second half of the year.

My argument here is that the market will indeed climb this wall of worry in the back half of the year. That's why I believe we are in a correction, and not in a bear market.

Let me start with that brick in the wall of worry.

How Durable Is the U.S. Equity Earnings Story?

Let's leave the elephant in the room (i.e., the oil shock due to the Hormuz blockade) for later.

So far, the S&P 500 has been able to absorb high interest rates, as economic activity in the U.S. was strong enough to protect earnings growth. In fact, as seen below, since mid-2025, the expectations for forward EPS growth have increased exponentially among S&P 500 constituents.

Therefore, recent developments like the one below make me increasingly concerned about the continuation of the current strong growth cycle.

The chart above is from the BEA's March 13 update, when real GDP growth in Q4 2025 was revised to 0.7% (annualized), down sharply from 4.4% in Q3.

Based on the BEA's second estimate, it appears that real final sales to private domestic purchasers rose only 1.9% in Q4 vs 2.4% in the advance estimate.

Out of all the line items that I monitor in the GDP estimate, final sales to private domestic purchasers are one of my go-to items for gauging the state of private sector demand.

Therefore, the chart above suggests that underlying domestic demand had already cooled before the oil shock hit in March. On top of that, January's real PCE increased by only 0.1% month over month. As a side note, the February 2026 PCE release is only scheduled for April 9, 2026. Why is that a problem?

Well, as seen below, the real PCE MoM change has slowed down since September last year and is now barely above 0%.

Again, based on the first chart in this section, it seems that the Street is quite bullish on continued demand resilience translating into earnings growth. The data I presented so far, however, suggests a slowdown in consumer spending prior (emphasis) to the oil shock.

Looking at unemployment now, February's nonfarm payrolls fell by 92,000 (after a January increase of 126,000), and the unemployment rate ticked up to 4.4%.

To be crystal clear, I am not worried about the unemployment rate right now in the U.S. In fact, one of my favorite indicators of a recession is the Sahm Rule Recession Indicator, which looks at the average U.S. unemployment rate over the last three months and compares it with the lowest three-month average from the past year.

For readers new to this metric, a simple way to interpret the figure is with this example: a reading of 0.27 (which is the latest value of this indicator) means the current three-month average unemployment rate is 0.27 percentage points above its lowest three-month average from the last 12 months.

A reading of 0.50 or higher is the threshold that signals recession risk under the Sahm Rule. Funny enough, the indicator in July, August, and September 2024 came in slightly above 0.5. That's when I started to (naively) think that AI adoption was behind the hike in unemployment.

It appears that I was dead wrong. There are many headlines (like the one below) that suggest that the hike in tech layoffs may be due to AI spending, not AI adoption.

Here is a very interesting remark from that article:

Promises of a leaner approach have been welcomed on Wall Street, especially from tech giants that are trying to justify huge amounts of capital spending to pay for the data centers, chips and other components required to power AI technology.

Back to unemployment, what concerns me is the trend, and the fact that the AI adoption may not really be the driver behind the higher unemployment in the last two years, versus the start of the AI supercycle back in early 2023 (or late 2022, if you were really switched on with the AI industry).

Overall, outside of a few companies that are clearly benefiting from AI adoption, like Palantir or the big two LLM startups (OpenAI and Anthropic), I remain skeptical of a near-term broad adoption of AI agents, particularly in the enterprise segment.

To be clear, this may happen in 3-5 years, but it ain't happening today. This means that AI-driven earnings growth remains limited for the majority of companies if the macro backdrop continues to deteriorate this year.

Why I'm Not A Bear

I strongly believe that by the second half of the year, investors will have largely forgotten about this growth concern if the following two conditions are both satisfied.

The Strait Of Hormuz "Blockade"

First, the Strait of Hormuz needs to reopen (fast). Headlines like the one below make me increasingly concerned that a quick reopening of the strait is unlikely in the next 1-2 weeks.

Yes, yes, yes. I know. There is often a gap between what Trump says and what he does.

However, the conflict in the Middle East is far from de-escalating, especially after Iran's latest attack on an oil tanker off the coast of Dubai, following Trump's recent warnings that the U.S. will obliterate the country's main energy plants if it does not agree to a peace deal and open the Strait of Hormuz.

If I look at the data, tanker transits are far below the historical average, despite the fact that Trump said Iran agreed to allow 20 oil tankers through the Strait of Hormuz starting this Monday.

That said, just like last year, I believe this exogenous event may come to a quick end, as it's not in the interest of any of the parties involved to keep the Strait closed for an extended period of time.

If I had to make my own call, I would say the Strait of Hormuz reopens in 2-3 weeks, with positive talks between both parties before the April 6 deadline.

If you think I may be too optimistic, bear in mind that some analysts are projecting a normalization by early April:

Notwithstanding uncertainty about the ceasefire negotiations, in our base case, ‌we ⁠expect traffic through the Strait to normalize by early April, which would be consistent with Brent averaging $85/b in 2026.- Barclays

Once this overhang is removed from investors' minds, I foresee another bull run, similar to what happened last year. The continuation of which will largely depend on the second factor.

Warsh Needs to Aggressively Cut Rates

Despite the data that I presented in the last section, in its last SEP, the Fed increased its economic growth forecast for 2026 to 2.4% vs 2.3% in December. On top of that, they left the unemployment rate unchanged to 4.4%, forecasting a decrease to 4.3% in 2027.

The bears are arguing that the market is no longer projecting rate cuts this year, with some estimates suggesting nearly a 30% chance of a hike this year.

I think these estimates are dead wrong. There are two reasons why.

First, the Fed tends to look through oil shocks, as they tend to be transitory in nature and have a limited impact on core inflation (which excludes food and energy). Here is a remark from Chairman Powell during the last FOMC meeting:

So the first thing is, there is the original thinking—the long-standing thinking, put it that way, is that you do “look through” energy shocks...

In fact, during his appearance this week at Harvard University, Powell leaned again on a wait-and-see approach amid this geopolitical uncertainty:

We ‌feel like our policy's in a good place for us to wait and see how that turns out

My second argument is that, despite the headlines, the Fed is still looking at a rate cut this year.

In the latest dot plot chart, the expectation was still largely for one rate cut in 2026, with little dispersion across FOMC participants:

Bottom line, I think Kevin Warsh still has a case to cut interest rates this year as once his nomination goes through and he takes over from Powell in May this year. By June, I expect the oil shock to fade, with Brent likely in the mid $70s and the S&P 500 on track to retest new highs.

As I explained in many of my macro articles, I think Warsh will be dovish. My rationale is (annoyingly) simple. Here is an excerpt from one of my articles:

I’m not worried in the slightest about rate cuts in the second half of the year. In fact, I wouldn't completely rule out a jumbo cut (that is, 50 bps) in the June meeting, likely driven by Trump’s pressure on Warsh to lower interest rates.

In my view, the lowest hanging fruit for the Republicans to increase their chances of winning the mid-term elections is tackling the affordability problem in the U.S., starting with lower mortgage rates (which, by the way, have increased 40 bps since the war started):

Prediction markets suggest that odds for the mid-term elections are in favor of the Democratic Party, which means that the Trump administration needs to do something quite impactful to gain votes. And, in my view, that may be lowering interest rates drastically, beyond what the data may suggest.

Looking Ahead

In the short term, I prefer to remain on the sidelines as there are no solid signs that suggest the end of the conflict.

Therefore, I see days like today as a dead cat bounce, despite the solid market breadth and returns across pretty much all the industries within the S&P 500:

From a price action perspective, I still believe the S&P 500 may see 6,150 in H1 this year, especially if next week, the (indirect) talks between the U.S. and Iran don't lead to a clear reopening of the Strait of Hormuz.

That said, in the longer term, I remain constructive, despite the poor economic data that I presented in the first section of this article.

My main point is that the oil shock appears to be transitory, and the Fed will likely adopt a dovish stance, starting in June this year.

That said, if policy easing is delayed (which is what the market is currently pricing in), the stretched earnings expectations that I discussed in this article become harder to defend, and I wouldn't be surprised to see the S&P 500 selling off a second time this year.

data.title: First Solar: Sold Out U.S. Solar Leader At An Attractive Discount
data.description:
Intro

Given the ongoing Iran crisis and the resulting surge in oil prices, it’s natural for investors to look for potential beneficiaries in this elevated energy pricing environment. The most direct winners are, of course, oil producers, whose topline improves when crude prices rise. Oil is a global commodity, and although there are many regional blends priced differently, overall prices tend to advance and decline broadly in sync.

Natural gas, however, behaves differently. The market has been heavily disrupted as Qatar’s LNG exports are constrained, and roughly 20% of global LNG supply is effectively cut off due to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. Unlike oil, gas is transported around the world largely in the form of LNG, which means the ability to export depends on liquefaction capacity. In the United States, only about 14% of natural gas production can be liquefied and shipped to world markets.

Why does this matter when looking at the solar panel giant First Solar right now? The fact is, natural gas remains the main source of electricity generation in the U.S. Since U.S. gas prices do not move in sync with global LNG markets, the higher global price environment hasn’t translated into higher domestic gas prices. Instead, U.S. natural gas prices have even declined slightly since the Iran crisis began, while global gas prices have surged more than 50%.

EIA forecast for 2024–2027 US electricity generation by source:

FSLR’s position in that environment

This means First Solar, which primarily sells utility-scale solar modules domestically in the U.S., does not directly benefit from rising global gas prices. At first glance, it might seem like a winner in a high‑energy‑price environment, as it provides an alternative to traditional fossils, but in reality, it isn’t — at least not directly or in the short term.

Furthermore, after looking closely at First Solar, I found that their production is actually fully booked for the next three years. That means there’s no immediate benefit to the company from higher energy prices, even if the increased cost of fossil fuels could lead to greater demand for solar modules. They just don't have any available capacity to produce and sell more.

However, with the Iran/Hormuz crisis triggering a broad market selloff, including First Solar’s stock, it might be worth taking a closer look for a potential long-term secular winner available at a discounted price right now.

First Solar in Brief

Founded in 1999, First Solar is the leading U.S. solar technology company. The company stands out among the world’s ten largest solar manufacturers as the only one headquartered in the United States and the only major player that does not operate manufacturing facilities in China.

First Solar’s advanced thin‑film photovoltaic (PV) technology, developed in its R&D centers in California and Ohio, represents a new generation of solar solutions, offering a competitive, high‑performance, and lower‑carbon alternative to traditional crystalline‑silicon panels.

First Solar operates five manufacturing facilities in the United States — three in Ohio, one in Alabama, and one in Louisiana. These plants are expected to provide more than 17 GW of annual domestic manufacturing capacity once fully ramped. Globally, the company anticipates reaching approximately 25 GW of annual production capacity by 2026, supported by additional manufacturing sites in India, Malaysia, and Vietnam.

The company describes its manufacturing process as follows:

First Solar thin film PV modules are produced using a fully integrated, continuous process under one roof that does not rely on Chinese crystalline silicon (c-Si) supply chains. Its proprietary, vertically integrated process transforms sheets of glass into fully functioning solar panels in approximately four hours with a combination of highly skilled workers, Industry 4.0 architecture, machine-to-machine communication, artificial intelligence, and Internet of Things connectivity.

Advantages of First Solar technology over Chinese crystalline silicon (c-Si):

Each module features a layer of Cadmium Telluride (CdTe) semiconductor, derived from byproducts of copper and zinc mining, which boasts a number of qualities over conventional c-Si, including lower cost, superior scalability, and a higher theoretical efficiency limit.

First Solar’s thin film PV technology produces energy-efficient modules with a superior degradation rate, temperature coefficient, spectral and shading response, and the smallest environmental footprint in the industry. First Solar's thin film modules require only 1-2% of the semiconductor material needed by traditional c-Si modules to produce a comparable amount of power.

First Solar is the U.S. market leader, holding 12% of the utility solar market share, and the market is projected to grow by an average of 5.3% annually through 2034:

Q4 FY 2025 Earnings and 2026 Outlook

Q4 EPS missed expectations by $0.40, while revenue beat by $114 million.

Outlook fell well short of analysts’ expectations, coming in significantly below the $6.16 billion consensus estimate, with an estimated 17.0–18.2 GW of modules sold. The GAAP profit outlook wasn’t provided due to uncertainty surrounding tax credit discounts and other factors affecting the accounting.

The weak 2026 outlook has forced analysts to meaningfully reduce their expectations going forward:

The market reacted very negatively to the results, with First Solar's stock dropping nearly 15% the next trading day.

Valuation

To evaluate First Solar’s value, in my view, the forward P/E multiple gives a good read, as the company has $2.8 billion in cash on the balance sheet and only about $500 million in short‑ and long‑term debt.

With forward P/E multiples sitting at very low levels, ranging between 5 and 10 for the next five years, signaling clear undervaluation, it’s natural to wonder why. It could be at least partially blamed since the company issued a weak outlook and analysts cut their forecasts significantly, but it still seems, in my view, disproportionately discounted.

Why Market Discounts First Solar So Deeply

The reason is the transferable tax credits from the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA). These 45X advanced manufacturing production tax credits are generated by the company’s U.S.-based manufacturing facilities. In 2025, the company cashed in about $1.6 billion from these credits and expects to make an additional $2.10 to $2.19 billion from the Section 45X tax credit in 2026. So, if we look at 2026 EBITDA guidance, it is estimated to be $500 million to $590 million without these credits. With the 2026 capex projected to be in the range of $800 million to $1 billion, it means the company would actually lose money without government incentives.

So, these low P/E multiples are only relevant if the tax credit system remains unchanged going forward.

The explanation of these credits and their maturity:

The 45X Credit, established by the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022, is effective for components produced and sold after December 2022 but, for components other than critical minerals, will phase out starting in 2030 and terminate completely by 2033. The 45X Credit is designed to strengthen U.S. energy security by incentivizing the onshoring of production of key clean energy components and critical minerals.

The big question is, what happens when these credits start shrinking in 2030 and vanish entirely by 2033? Nobody really knows, and that uncertainty is probably the biggest reason for the steep discount in First Solar’s stock price right now. As shown in the Seeking Alpha analyst estimate table above, coverage dropped from 17 analysts in 2028 to just 6 in 2030, likely reflecting the unpredictability.

What we do know, listening to the latest earnings call and analysts' questions around the topic, is that normal gross margin without government tax credits is considered to be around 20%. If the company’s revenue is expected to reach $7.2 billion by 2030, that could translate to roughly $1.5 billion in gross margin. With other operating costs averaging around 10% of revenue, as it has been, a quick back-of-the-envelope calculation suggests about $700 million in operating profit. Plus, the 2030 tax incentives won’t be phased out yet, only starting to gradually decrease. I think while it’s tough to make truly accurate predictions, I could still expect profits to remain at least in the range of $700 million to $1.5 billion in 2030-2033, even if no new supportive tax measures are introduced by the U.S. administration.

Given First Solar’s unique position as the only U.S. solar cell producer at this scale, I believe the current restrictions on foreign imports are likely to remain, and even without administration tax support, this could create a strong pricing environment, enabling the company to enjoy better margins.

The Market Trends in Favor of First Solar

According to Electrek, writing at the end of 2025:

Renewables make up 26% of US generation

The mix of all renewables (wind, solar, hydropower, biomass, and geothermal) produced 8.7% more electricity in January-November than a year earlier and accounted for 25.7% of total US electricity production, up from 24.3% 12 months earlier.

Renewables’ share of electrical generation is now second to only that of natural gas, whose electrical output actually dropped by 3.7% during the first 11 months of 2025.

As reported by IEA, the overall electricity demand grows, and renewables are getting an extra boost from the decline in coal-fired capacity:

In 2026, U.S. coal generation declines by 7% in our forecast as generation from renewable sources increases and the electric power sector retires about 4% of its coal-fired generating capacity.

EIA foresees continued strong solar growth, with 41.5 GW of utility-scale solar capacity being added by the end of January 2027..

...and the growth of fossils is expected to stall:

.. forecasted growth for fossil fuels and nuclear power is even bleaker than that experienced last year. Zero growth is currently projected for nuclear power while net fossil fuel capacity is expected to fall by 4,261.3 MW.

Thus, during the coming 12 months, renewables and battery storage will account for all net new utility-scale capacity additions.

From a purely economic standpoint, the additional key factor is the cost of producing kWh without subsidies. In that comparison, solar, along with wind, comes out on top, according to Lazard:

In a base comparison, utility-scale solar and wind have the lowest LCOE of all sources. Utility-scale solar ranges from $0.038/kWh to $0.217/kWh, while onshore wind registers the lowest possible LCOE over the narrowest range, from $0.037/kWh to $0.086/kWh. Offshore wind’s LCOE ranges between $72/MWh and $140/MWh.

For comparison, under the same criteria, gas peaking costs $0.149/kWh to $0.251/kWh, nuclear is $0.141/kWh to $0.220/kWh, coal is $0.071/kWh to $0.173/kWh, and gas combined cycle is $0.048/kWh to $0.109/kWh,

In short, while headlines focus on the current US administration’s fossil-fuel-friendly stance and the widespread belief that natural gas will be the main source to power the growing energy needs of AI-driven data centers, the reality paints a bit different picture. Utilities are shifting to renewables, and it seems to be the only segment of power generation expected to grow heading into 2026.

Risks

The main risk, as the company itself has noted, is regulatory uncertainty. While this could eventually turn out to be a net positive, the many variables involved make it tough for management to chart long-term strategies in such conditions. That said, its scale, unique technology, and leadership position in the U.S.—especially given widely communicated policies aimed at reducing Chinese exposure in solar markets—give the company a strong hand in dealings with the government.

After listening to the earnings call, it seems clear that the company’s international operations are becoming a meaningful concern as well. As management discussed, the India subsidiary has found its footing in the domestic market—operating at full utilization and roughly breakeven—but the Vietnam and Malaysia facilities tell a very different story. These factories are significantly underutilized, currently running at only about 20% capacity.

Management largely attributes this to ongoing uncertainty around tariffs and other regulatory policies. However, it also appears that the company has not yet identified the right strategic role for these overseas factories. Until that happens, these operations represent an additional layer of risk for the business, as I see it.

Takeaway

While looking for beneficiaries of high energy prices, I came across what I see as a clear energy sector secular winner: First Solar. It is essentially the only large‑scale U.S.‑based solar technology manufacturer with unique, IP‑protected technology, which gives it a rare and strategically important position in the market.

The main reason the company is not trading at higher multiples is understandable. Once the current tax credits expire in 2033, there’s uncertainty about how the political landscape and policies might develop. However, both parties have seemingly agreed on one point: the U.S. must reduce its dependence on China‑linked supply chains, especially in strategic sectors like energy.

At the same time, electricity demand is rising —driven by hyperscale AI data centers, and solar remains one of the fastest‑growing energy sources. First Solar continues to evolve to become more efficient and cost‑effective at scale. I think this creates a realistic path for the company to remain profitable even without government incentives.

I rate First Solar as a Buy at current levels.
